questions.question: The school of psychology known as functionalism, which emphasized the purposes of the mind’s operations, was founded by:
The fact that you know you have seen the face before but cannot remember the name of the person, illustrates the cognitive process of:
The term _________ refers to the relevance of the research to the “real world.”
_______________ challenged the assumption that mental events were beyond the realm of scientific study.
Since the 1970s, various techniques of _________ have allowed us to construct pictures of the anatomy and functioning of intact brains.
Historians date the founding of scientific psychology to the 1879 laboratory of:
The term “limited capacity processors” suggests that:
options: human beings can only do so many things at once
neurons can only fire at a certain rate
processing in the brain occurs in parallel
questions.correct_answer: human beings can only do so many things at once

questions.question: This psychologist sought to describe the intellectual structures underlying cognitive experience at different developmental points through an approach he called genetic epistemology:
Clinical interviews are to introspection as ______ are to __________.
Which of the following is a basic assumption of the connectionist approach?
options: multiple stores where information is kept throughout processing
networks of connections among simple processing units
a central processor that directs the flow of information
questions.correct_answer: networks of connections among simple processing units

questions.question: A toy on the table represents:
A patient with prosopagnosia has difficulty recognizing:

When you let go of a balloon and it floats away from you into the sky, the size of the retinal image gets smaller and smaller. However, you do not perceive the balloon as shrinking. This is an example of:
The meaningful interpretation of a proximal stimulus is called the:
The two best studied forms of perception are:
Which of the following represents a good example of a proximal stimulus?
options: the retinal image formed by a tree.
questions.correct_answer: the retinal image formed by a tree.

questions.question: According to Gibson’s theory, the acts or behaviors permitted by objects, places, and events are called:
A stencil provides a good analogy for the theory of:
___________ are to visual perception what phonemes are to language, according to Biederman.
The principle governing reversible figures is:
According to Attenuation theory of Treisman, people tend to process:
options: only to the level of physical characteristics.
only to the level of linguistic characteristics, separating it into words.
at a semantic level, analyzing for meaning most of the time.
only as much as is necessary to separate the attended from the unattended message
questions.correct_answer: only as much as is necessary to separate the attended from the unattended message

questions.question: The “cocktail party effect” refers to the fact that shadowing performance is disrupted when _______ is embedded in the unattended message.
In Kahneman’s model of attention, allocation of mental resources is affected by preferences for certain kinds of tasks over others. These preferences are known as:
The word “cat” is ______ by the phrase “The dog chased the….” That is, the word cat is especially ready to be recognized or attended to.
When listening to a conversation, your attention is momentarily diverted when you hear your name spoken in a different conversation across the room. This is an example of the:
Which of the following would NOT be a reasonable basis for filtering, according to Broadbent’s model?
options: whether the message was coming from your right or your left side
the pitch of the voice reading the message
the loudness of the voice reading the message
the language that the message was being read in
questions.correct_answer: the language that the message was being read in

questions.question: Cognitive psychologists use all of the following as criteria for determining whether an activity is automatic EXCEPT:
options: whether it gives rise to conscious awareness.
whether it interferes with other activities.

questions.question: Certain stimuli seem to jump off the page at the viewer, causing an involuntary shift of attention that is referred to as:
Stroop interference lessens when:
options: participants are given more practice at naming colors.
participants are girls rather than boys.
participants are encouraged to focus carefully.
questions.correct_answer: participants are given more practice at naming colors.

questions.question: Research on divided attention suggests that:
options: some people can multitask without any drop in performance.
there are no limits on the number of things that we can successfully do at once.
as individual tasks become more demanding, multitasking becomes more efficient.
if you think that you are doing two things simultaneously, you are probably really rapidly switching attention back and forth between the two.
questions.correct_answer: if you think that you are doing two things simultaneously, you are probably really rapidly switching attention back and forth between the two.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of Baddeley’s working memory model?
Sternberg’s classic work on searching for information from short-term memory indicated that the search process is:
In the absence of rehearsal, short-term memory tends to:
The suffix effect relates to which type of memory?
Unattended information is stored briefly in:
Retrieval involves:
options: the translation of information into a form that can be stored
the storage of information over time
the calling to mind of previously stored information
questions.correct_answer: the calling to mind of previously stored information

questions.question: The primacy and recency effects in memory:
options: are thought to be due to the action of short-term memory
are thought to be due to the action of long-term memory
are thought to be due to the action of sensory memory
can be independently manipulated, indicating at least two types of memory at work
questions.correct_answer: can be independently manipulated, indicating at least two types of memory at work

questions.question: Information in short-term memory is assumed to be coded primarily by:
Repeating a phone number to yourself to hold it in memory while you dial it would use which component of working memory?
options: both the visuospatial sketchpad and the phonological loop

questions.question: Disrupting the process of long-term potentiation leads to:
As classically conceived, long-term memory is held to have all of the following properties EXCEPT:
options: It comprises a permanent or at least semipermanent store.
Information stored within it is not always easily accessible.

questions.question: Psychologists believe that the capacity of long-term memory is:
Which of the following are most likely to be confused in long-term memory?
The code in long-term memory is based on:
Your memory for how to ride a bicycle is an example of ______ memory.
A retrieval cue will be most effective when it is highly distinctive or unusual, according to the principle of:
Context effects and state-dependent learning effects occur:
Bahrick’s study of retention of Spanish vocabulary words showed that large portions of information remained in long-term memory for:
Techniques designed to improve memory, often involving the use of visual imagery, are called:
Your memory of your first college lecture would be an example of:
According to the notion of cognitive economy, a characteristic like “has wings” would be stored along with which of the following semantic memory nodes?
Priming in lexical decision tasks may be explained by the idea of:
Which of the following is a good example of a superordinate level of categorization?
The schema view of concept formation assumes that:
options: There are clear boundaries among individual schemata.
There is cognitive economy among concepts.
No information is stored about actual instances.

questions.question: According to research by Collins and Quillian, the statement “Siamese cats have blue eyes” will be verified:
options: slower than “Siamese cats give birth to live young.”
faster than “Siamese cats give birth to live young.”
in the same amount of time as “Siamese cats give birth to live young.”
questions.correct_answer: faster than “Siamese cats give birth to live young.”

questions.question: Conrad has found evidence that the statement “A shark can move” can be verified in the same amount of time as “An animal can move.” These results suggest that reaction time is best predicted by:
Which of the following would be a part of your declarative memory system?
options: knowing how to ride a bicycle.
knowing how to drive a car.
knowing how to react to a red light.
being able to name a hybrid car.
questions.correct_answer: being able to name a hybrid car.

questions.question: Collins and Loftus’s spreading activation theory differs from the hierarchical network theory in that:
options: it dispenses with the idea of cognitive economy.
it relies on the assumption of hierarchical structure.
it cannot account for the typicality effect.
it makes stronger predictions than hierarchical models.
questions.correct_answer: it dispenses with the idea of cognitive economy.

questions.question: “Characteristic features” and “family resemblance” are important aspects of the _________ view of concepts.
Studies of semantic memory have shown that in a lexical decision task, people are faster at responding to the stimulus “bread” if it is paired with a stimulus such as:
According to the ______ view of concepts, people categorize new instances by comparing them to representations of previously stored instances.
According to the typicality effect, the statement “A dog is a household pet” should be verified:
options: faster than “A poodle is a household pet.”
faster than “A dog is a living thing.”
faster than “A ferret is a household pet.”
faster than “A dog is an animal.”
questions.correct_answer: faster than “A ferret is a household pet.”

questions.question: Which of the following would be considered an example of a “natural kind” concept?
A schema for a routine event, such as going to the dentist, is called a:
A ________ is a mental representation of some object, event, or pattern.
A ______ can be defined as a class of similar things that share either an essential core, or some similarity in perceptual, biological, or functional properties.
Which of the following is NOT TRUE of the classical view of concepts?
options: It proposes that concepts are mentally represented by lists of features.
It assumes that membership in a category is clear-cut.
It accurately predicts the typicality effect.
“Necessary” and “sufficient” features play an important role in the theory.
questions.correct_answer: It accurately predicts the typicality effect.

questions.question: Which of the following is a good example of a basic level of categorization?
If “soda” is a basic-level category, then ______ would be a subordinate level.
The ______ view of concepts argues that a person uses his/her theories about the way the world works to justify the classification of instances in the same category.
Participants doing mental imagery tasks tend to show brain activity in the:
How do people represent and navigate in and through space? This is a question of
Images can prime the visual pathway, making it easier to detect a faint stimulus. This is an example of:
Which of the following questions would take you the LONGEST TIME to answer?
options: Which is faster, a turtle or a sports car?
Which is faster, a turtle or a cheetah?
Which is faster, a snail or a caterpillar?
Which is faster, a caterpillar or a cat?
questions.correct_answer: Which is faster, a snail or a caterpillar?

questions.question: The relational-organizational hypothesis is supported by:
options: the effectiveness of the method of loci.
the fact that concrete words are recalled better than abstract words.
the fact that noninteractive images do not facilitate recall whereas interactive images do facilitate recall.
the effectiveness of the pegword method.
questions.correct_answer: the fact that noninteractive images do not facilitate recall whereas interactive images do facilitate recall.

questions.question: Paivio’s ____ hypothesis argues that long-term memory contains two separate systems that represent information in verbal and visual forms, respectively.
Which of the following methods would most help you to remember the word pair “elephant-cigar”?
options: repeating the word “elephant” over and over while visualizing a cigar
visualizing an elephant smoking a cigar
repeating both words over and over to yourself
visualizing an elephant and a cigar, not touching each other
questions.correct_answer: visualizing an elephant smoking a cigar

questions.question: Your mental map of your campus is probably:
options: more regular, with more straight lines and right angles, than reality.
less regular, with fewer straight lines and right angles, than reality
questions.correct_answer: more regular, with more straight lines and right angles, than reality.

questions.question: Finke’s principle of _____ states that mental imagery allows us to retrieve information that was not intentionally stored.
A propositional representation is thought to be ______ in nature.
Which of the following is/are generally agreed upon as necessary criteria/criterion for claiming that a communication system is a language?
Infinite combinations of ideas can be expressed in language. In other words, language is:
A fundamental problem of speech perception, according to Miller, is that:
options: speech is discrete rather than continuous.
a single phoneme sounds different depending on its context.
hearing is a less accurate sense than vision.
missing phonemes can render words incomprehensible.
questions.correct_answer: a single phoneme sounds different depending on its context.

questions.question: The branch of linguistics devoted to the study of meaning:
Did she say “many” or “men knee”? This type of ambiguity is referred to as:
The underlying knowledge that allows people to produce and comprehend their language is called:
The study of the ways in which sounds can be combined in any given language is called:
We notice ambiguities in sentences:
options: when they make no sense at all.

questions.question: According to research, which of the following sets of sentences would take the longest amount of time to read and comprehend?
options: We took the dog to the vet. The dog was nervous.
Sara drove the car to the market. The car ran out of gas.
She wrapped the Christmas presents. The sweater needed a larger box.
We got some beer out of the car. The beer was warm
questions.correct_answer: She wrapped the Christmas presents. The sweater needed a larger box.

questions.question: Speech acts in which the utterance itself is the action—such as “You’re fired!”—are considered to be which type of speech act?
_____ is defined as “going beyond the information given.”
Which of the following is an example of an ill-defined problem?
options: putting together your schedule of classes for next semester
solving the Tower of Hanoi problem
questions.correct_answer: putting together your schedule of classes for next semester

questions.question: The problem-solving technique of ______ involves comparing the goal with the starting point, thinking of possible ways to overcome the distance between them, and choosing the best one.
Mental set:
options: can only be induced by large amounts of practice.
can lead us to make unwarranted assumptions about a problem.
involves the use of analogy to solve a problem.
aids problem solving because it supplies an efficient problem representation
questions.correct_answer: can lead us to make unwarranted assumptions about a problem.

questions.question: When one recalls the solution to an old problem and uses it to solve a new, similarly structured problem, one has reasoned by:
______ is a very important technique for solving the Towers of Hanoi problem.
Which of the following is the best example of a well-defined problem?
options: creating a guest list for a party
finding a detour around a traffic problem
writing a paper for your cognition class

questions.question: Psychologists focus on well-defined problems because:
options: they often take weeks or months to solve.
they can be complicated to score.

questions.question: Adoption of a rigid mental set toward an object is called:
The General Problem Solver (GPS) is a computer program that solves problems in crypt arithmetic and logic using:
Deductive reasoning involves which of the following?
options: going from general to specific statements
conclusions that add new information to what is given
going from specific to general statements
going from specific to specific statements
questions.correct_answer: going from general to specific statements

questions.question: If a person has five letters in his or her name, then s/he is a Glirple. I can now validly assume that:
options: Shawn is a Glirple, and anyone who is not a Glirple cannot be named Susan.
anyone who is a Glirple must have five letters in his/her name.
anyone who is not a Glirple cannot be named Susan.
questions.correct_answer: Shawn is a Glirple, and anyone who is not a Glirple cannot be named Susan.

questions.question: When we assess probability by judging the ease with which relevant examples come to mind, we are using the heuristic of:
Which type of reasoning results in conclusions that contain new information?
If performed correctly, deductive reasoning can be said to have ______. This means that it is impossible for the premises to be true and the conclusion to be false.
If a person has five letters in his/her name, then s/he is a Glirple. Angie now reasons that Fred cannot possibly be a Glirple. Angie has:
options: made a correct inference according to modus ponens.
made a correct inference according to modus tollens.
committed the fallacy of denying the antecedent.
committed the fallacy of affirming the consequent.
questions.correct_answer: committed the fallacy of denying the antecedent.

questions.question: When Wason’s four-card task is replaced with an everyday problem about checking the identification of people who are drinking Coke and beer.
options: people still neglect modus tollens in their responses.
people turn over all four cards instead of the two that are necessary and sufficient
people turn over only one card instead of the two that are necessary and sufficient.
about 75% of participants solve the problem correctly.
questions.correct_answer: about 75% of participants solve the problem correctly.

questions.question: Some members of the Jones family are tall. Some tall people play basketball. Which of the following is true?
options: Logically, some members of the Jones family play basketball.
Logically, no members of the Jones family play basketball.
Most people assume that some members of the Jones family play basketball.
Most people assume that all members of the Jones family play basketball.
questions.correct_answer: Most people assume that some members of the Jones family play basketball.

questions.question: The ease of reasoning about an analogy depends on all of these factors EXCEPT:
options: the difficulty of comprehending the individual terms in the analogy.
the reasoner’s knowledge about the individual terms.
the difficulty of finding a relationship between the first two terms.
the reasoner’s amount of training in formal reasoning procedures.
questions.correct_answer: the reasoner’s amount of training in formal reasoning procedures.

questions.question: Which of the following is a difference between everyday reasoning and formal reasoning?
options: In everyday reasoning, all premises are usually supplied.
In everyday reasoning, there is typically one correct answer.
In everyday reasoning, problems are solved as a means of achieving other goals.
In formal reasoning, problems are not self-contained.
questions.correct_answer: In everyday reasoning, problems are solved as a means of achieving other goals.

questions.question: The gambler’s fallacy is thought of as a special instance of:
options: belief in the law of small numbers.
questions.correct_answer: belief in the law of small numbers.

questions.question: You have just spent 10 minutes trying to figure out the answer to a single problem on your math quiz. In spite of your lack of success, you continue to struggle, neglecting to continue on to other problems because you’ve already invested so much time and effort in this problem. You have fallen victim to:
Normative models of decision making describe:
options: how we ought to make decisions in realistic circumstances.
what people actually do when they make decisions.

questions.question: Past events seem “inevitable” due to the action of the:

Anita is blonde, extremely attractive, and lives in an expensive condo. If we judge the probability of Anita’s being a model as quite high because she resembles our stereotype of a model, we are using the heuristic of:
The mental activities that take place in choosing among alternatives are collectively called:
The tendency to seek out information that supports our current beliefs is called:
Which of the following is NOT one of the five phases of decision making, according to Galotti?
According to psychologists, human decision making often falls short of optimality because:
options: there is not enough information available to make a rational decision.
one cannot be rational under conditions of uncertainty.
the information available overwhelms the cognitive processes.
rationality assumes a higher degree of intelligence than most people have.
questions.correct_answer: the information available overwhelms the cognitive processes.

questions.question: The phase of decision making that involves finding a way to organize information is called:
